All right, you're back. So that's my second call, no sale made. Now I'll wait anywhere from another day to three days, depending on what's going on in the industry in the year, you know, just the individual factors there in terms of, you know, what's the appropriate, it's a bit of feel here. In other words, I could wait two days, I could wait three days, I wouldn't wait a week or two though, that I wouldn't do. I want to catch them while this is still fresh in their mind, they've just made their purchase. Now, why would I do that? They could disturb for a second, you say, well, they really haven't had a chance to experience all the benefits. Why not wait? Well, there are multiple reasons for that. And I'll give you the three most important ones with the first one being, why should you? There's no reason to wait. There's no law, as I said, there's no steadfast rule that says you have to wait a certain amount of time in arbitrary period until you can go back to a person and sell them something else, either more of the first sale and upsell and then tie different product in the form of a cross sell or for that matter, ask for a referral. You don't have to. Okay, that's number one. Number two, you ever hear the expression out of sight, out of mind? Yeah, well, that applies to this. In other words, you just met somebody, you wanted to start seeing you and hearing from you and the more regularly the better. It's the same reason why that on the online communities of the world, the people who get big followings are the ones who keep posting content on a consistent basis. The more someone sees you, the more someone hears from you, the more you stay freshen them up and frankly, the more they start to trust you, rightly or wrongly. That's just the way it is. So if you sell someone a product on Monday and then you call them back on a Tuesday just to check in how you do everything's great, then you call them back again on a Thursday to sell them something. They're like, Oh, wow, this guy's a new fixture in my life. That is just not a negative as long as what you're selling them is going to benefit them and also as long as they're having a positive experience with what you first sold them. Now, if there are problems with the first sale, that's okay too. As long as they're being addressed by you, in fact, you can even strengthen the relationship. If something goes wrong, as long as it's not something wild, right? Like you just misrepresent it, but if something like, you know, basic goes wrong and these little things that you can fix, they'll say, wow, man, this person's on the ball. Okay. Now, let me just give you a little tip for that because this happens all the time when you sell someone a product or a service and they love it. It's all going well, but there's one thing that's not clear. Maybe a little bit of a login problem where they can't access one particular aspect of what you sold them. So what do you do? Well, I personally, I strongly recommend you do this is rather than trying to pass it off to someone else or a customer service success person, right? I want you to handle it yourself, resolve it yourself. And when the person says to you, your new client goes, wow, you know, thank you so much. I really appreciate you going above and beyond like that. Don't say to them, ah, it's no big deal. If you do that, you just lost all the currency. It says she, the reciprocity currency, so to speak, by diminishing what you did. So you're not going to say, oh, no, it's nothing. Or you're not going to say to them, yeah, well, you know, now you owe me pal, right? What you want to say to them is, oh, listen, it's what we do for all good clients. I'm sure you would do the same for me. You get it? I'm sure you'd extend the same for me. That's a marker. In a very nice way, you've created a marker for reciprocity. That's what we do with all good clients like family. I'm sure you do the same thing for me. I'll say, oh, yeah, of course I would. Exactly. Boom. Lock that down tight. So there's a really powerful way to actually strengthen the relationship in the short term by actually capitalizing on a minor problem. You're the guy or the girl that showed up. You fixed it. Boom. You look really good in this person's eyes. That goes a long way towards establishing that true trust at that ultra-high level. And once you have that, it's really a matter of does your product make sense to them? Meaning is that first ten at a really high level and can they afford it? Because your second ten and by default then because the company stands behind the product and you, you're there. As long as you're there, the third ten almost falls into line by default where it's at a really high level, the second ten. So then it's really about does the product itself make sense? Does it feel their need? Much easier close. Make sense? Okay, great. So that's the second reason why. And the third reason why is that there's this psychological aspect to all buyers that we like to confirm that we made a good decision. Those by buying again, we're almost confirming that our first decision was indeed the correct one. It makes us feel better about our prior decisions. That's called consistency. We create consistency by acting in the same way again and again. Now, let me give you an appropriate example of this. I was in the mortgage industry. I had a mortgage company that I owned. I had about 35, 40 salesmen working for me and we were making a ton of money. It was in the refi boom, very, very easy to make money. Right? I was going out into the field myself on a daily basis, going door to door, knocking on people's doors at homes and offices and storefronts and asked for the owner, hey, is the owner around? Hey, what were you paying on your mortgage? Just figuring the most on a home. They said, oh, I'm paying AF. Oh my God, really? I could definitely save you a ton of money. Take out my book. That was that. And I used a two step process. I would say, okay, and how long you're living there? Well, okay, and we don't process much to balance, blah, blah, blah, unimportant. I'd qualify them. Well, based on what you just said, I could save you a ton of money. In fact, you live up a new Rochelle. Oh my God, I'm going to be in the area later tonight. Be happy to stop by when you're not working and take care of it. You're like, great. That was it. What a powerful way to make money. I had days I made over 100,000 in one day, going door to door, business to business. Now, here's the deal in terms of customers to life. Typical scenario is I would go to a home and when I got there, sometimes I would have met just one partner, the husband or the wife or whatever, right? The other one wasn't there or it was done through a call call. I had people getting leads to me as well. And I walk in very often when I walked into someone's home, even though they might have been pleasant to me out in the field, by the time I got into their home, the magic that I have of showing up using body language and such a personality, that had worn off. And I walk in and I would cross the threshold and they'd be downright hostile to me, literally, downright hostile, not wanting to answer my questions, basically saying there's no way under the sun that I'm doing my mortgage with you tonight. I don't know what you think you're doing here. You showed up in my house. Uh-uh, ain't happening. 15 minutes later, I'm like, oh, thank you for your pass with the golf, but the woman is cooking me a five course meal. Okay. The husband's cracking the champagne with me and the wife's on the phone to their cousins and uncles and aunts and everyone else. Oh my God, you have to meet this guy. He's the best mortgage broken road that happened to me all the time. Why? Think about that. I hadn't even done their mortgage yet. I had not solved their problem. All I've done is taken him through a sale, closed him, wrote up the application, but nothing had happened actually. They hadn't got their mortgage redone and they're calling people and getting referrals for me. Why is that? Because human nature, once we make that decision to trust, and I said this in the lesson and looping on the forest gunpan, well now it really carries through to getting referrals. You get it? This is why I said there's no arbitrary amount of time that has to go by to upsell, cross out or get referrals. Powerful. So make sure that whatever you're selling, the first thing you want to analyze is what is your customer path? Every industry and products within an industry have a best customer path. What do you get to sell them first? What do you get to sell them second? What do you get to sell them third? And also, of course, based on the individual client's needs, you'll have a variety of things and you'll sell them what best fits their need. But there's certain classes of products, certain price points that follow a logical progression from one sale to a fully developed client that has all of your products that fit this person. And eventually that's your goal. You want to sell this person everything that you can that helps them live a better and a more secure life, makes them feel in more control and happier person as well. That's when you're doing your job. You're essentially giving them value in all areas through upsells and cross sells, bottom line. Now, I want to stop here. I give you a lot of information. And when we come back, I'm going to start digging specifically into the actual patterns that you use to upsell and cross sell as well as talk to you about a specific type of sale I call the after sale, which is very prevalent in the auto industry and also in places where you have warranties and certain annals. In other words, you might sell someone a product yet the real money the company makes is in the warranty that they sell on the product, very common in the household goods world. Dishwashers, TV sets, these extra three year payment, three year warranty plans where you guarantee the product on top of the manufacturing warranty. I'll tell you a story in the next lesson that will blow you away. I was able to take out a massive billion dollar company, increase their close break by 32%, which is huge for a big company, but also increase their rate of conversion to warranties like a hundred full.
